result.markdown:
--- title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01 --- To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point. In order to perform the GIL16 vs GIL01 strains contrast at 60-mock minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2's key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time60"), c("Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL01.Time60")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5)) contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast) contrast_df <- contrast_df %>% { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>% left_join( annotation %>% filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>% dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source), by = "Gene_ID" ) %>% rename( Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean, LFC = log2FoldChange, Adjusted_p_value = padj ) %>% dplyr::select( Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source, Mean_normalized_counts, LFC, Adjusted_p_value ) ``` ::: As can be seen in the `summary()` results, 10 upregulated and 144 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 678 genes were discarded due to low counts, likely because they are expressed in other contrasts. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` out of 4990 with nonzero total read count adjusted p-value < 0.05 LFC > 0.58 (up) : 10, 0.2% LFC < -0.58 (down) : 144, 2.9% outliers [1] : 0, 0% low counts [2] : 678, 14% (mean count < 9) [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ], colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association", "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE, filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list( dom = "Brftip", buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"), fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2), scrollX = TRUE, scrollY = 400, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # MA plot A MA plot visualizes the relationship between the mean expression and the LFC for each gene. It helps to identify genes that are differentially expressed across conditions, highlighting those with large fold changes relative to their expression level. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} plotMA(contrast, alpha = 0.05, xlab = "Mean of normalized counts", ylab = "LFC") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](gil16_gil01_t60-mock_files/figure-html/ma_plot-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # DE genes DE genes are defined as those with an adjusted p-value below 0.05. Among them, upregulated genes are extracted as those with a LFC > 0.58, while downregulated genes are defined as those with an LFC < -0.58. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} up_genes <- rownames(subset(contrast, padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange > log2(1.5) & !is.na(padj))) down_genes <- rownames(subset(contrast, padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange < -log2(1.5) & !is.na(padj))) ``` ::: # Volcano plot A volcano plot displays each gene according to its LFC on the x-axis and the statistical significance (−log₁₀ adjusted p-value) on the y-axis. Among the upregulated genes, there are none from pBtic235 and GBJ002-DNT_117 stands out as the gene with the highest LFC. Downregulated genes are mostly from the chromosome, but there are also from pBtic235. In this case, group-specific protein (GBJ002_03815) and GBJ002-DNT_253 genes stand out as the chromosomal and pBtic235 genes, respectively, with the lowest LFC. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} volcano_plot(contrast_df) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Enrichment analysis In this section, we present the results of enrichment analyses based on the COG (Clusters of Orthologous Groups), KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes), GO (Gene Ontology) and EC (Enzyme Commission) categories for both upregulated and downregulated genes. ## COG According to COG categories, we only found negative enrichments in transcription (**K**) and function unknown (**S**) due to 14 and 8 DE genes, respectively. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} cog_up <- cog_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes) cog_down <- cog_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(cog_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", colnames = c( "COG", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(cog_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", colnames = c( "COG", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## KEGG According to KEGG categories, we observed negative enrichment in quorum sensing pathways due to RNA chaperone Hfq (GBJ002_00873), PlcR (GBJ002_02564), HTH cro/C1-type domain-containing protein (GBJ002_03361) and quorum-signaling peptide NprX (GBJ002_03362) genes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} kegg_up <- kegg_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes) kegg_down <- kegg_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(kegg_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 3) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(kegg_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 3) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## GO The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: **Biological Process** (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; **Molecular Function** (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and **Cellular Component** (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active. - Regarding **CC**, only positive enrichments were found, mainly related to the cytosol, ribosomes and ribonucleoprotein complex components. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} go_bp_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "BP") go_bp_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "BP") go_mf_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "MF") go_mf_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "MF") go_cc_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "CC") go_cc_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "CC") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(go_bp_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(go_bp_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(go_mf_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(go_mf_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(go_cc_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scrollY = 400, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(go_cc_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## EC The EC classification establishes up to four levels to define enzymes. In this section, the first three were analyzed, although no statistically significant results were obtained in any case. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} ec_1_up <- ec_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, 1) ec_1_down <- ec_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, 1) ec_2_up <- ec_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, 2) ec_2_down <- ec_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, 2) ec_3_up <- ec_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, 3) ec_3_down <- ec_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, 3) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(ec_1_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", colnames = c( "EC number", "Class", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(ec_1_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", colnames = c( "EC number", "Class", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(ec_2_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", colnames = c( "EC number", "Class", "Subclass", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(ec_2_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", colnames = c( "EC number", "Class", "Subclass", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(ec_3_up, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive enrichment", colnames = c( "EC number", "Class", "Subclass", "Sub-subclass", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(ec_3_down, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative enrichment", colnames = c( "EC number", "Class", "Subclass", "Sub-subclass", "DE", "No DE", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value"), extensions = "Scroller", options = list( scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Enrichment summary This table contains a summary of the enrichments for each category. The subcategory was used for KEGG and the description for GO. Although you can scroll, I recommend exporting using the Excel button and viewing the table in full screen. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} es_up <- enrichment_summary(up_genes, categories = list(cog_up, kegg_up, go_bp_up, go_mf_up, go_cc_up, ec_1_up, ec_2_up, ec_3_up)) es_down <- enrichment_summary(down_genes, categories = list(cog_down, kegg_down, go_bp_down, go_mf_down, go_cc_down, ec_1_down, ec_2_down, ec_3_down)) es_de <- rbind(es_up, es_down) rownames(es_de) <- c("UP", "DOWN") datatable(es_de, colnames = c("DE genes", "DE number", "COG", "KEGG", "GO (BP)", "GO (MF)", "GO (CC)", "EC (1)", "EC (2)", "EC (3)"), extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list( autoWidth = TRUE, dom = "Brftip", buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"), fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2), scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Tectivirus genes expression In this section, we modified the tectivirus genes names so that homologous genes could have their expression compared using DESeq2. Besides, we examined their expression using boxplots. The data are presented in **CPM** (counts per million), which indicates how many counts per million come from a given gene. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} tectivirus <- combine_tectivirus_genes() tectivirus_counts <- tectivirus$tectivirus_counts tectivirus_annotation <- tectivirus$tectivirus_annotation tectivirus_counts[] <- lapply(tectivirus_counts, function(x) if(is.numeric(x)) ifelse(x %% 1 == 0.5, ceiling(x), round(x)) else x) dds_tectivirus <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = tectivirus_counts, colData = metadata, design = ~ Replicate + Strain * Time + Strain * MMC) dds_tectivirus <- DESeq(dds_tectivirus) dds_tectivirus <- dds_tectivirus[grepl("^(g|dnt)", rownames(dds_tectivirus)), ] ``` ::: In order to perform the GIL16 vs GIL01 strains contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} contrast_tectivirus <- results(dds_tectivirus, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time60"), c("Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL01.Time60")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5)) contrast_tectivirus_df <- as.data.frame(contrast_tectivirus) contrast_tectivirus_df <- contrast_tectivirus_df %>% { x <- .; x$Gene_symbol <- rownames(x); x } %>% left_join( tectivirus_annotation, by = "Gene_symbol" ) %>% rename( Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean, LFC = log2FoldChange, Adjusted_p_value = padj ) %>% dplyr::select( Gene_symbol, Description, Type, Subtype, Source, Mean_normalized_counts, LFC, Adjusted_p_value ) ``` ::: As can be seen in the `summary()` results, only 1 upregulated gene was obtained. Below is an interactive table where this gene can be viewed individually. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} summary(contrast_tectivirus, alpha = 0.05) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` out of 27 with nonzero total read count adjusted p-value < 0.05 LFC > 0.58 (up) : 1, 3.7% LFC < -0.58 (down) : 0, 0% outliers [1] : 0, 0% low counts [2] : 0, 0% (mean count < 1651) [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} datatable(contrast_tectivirus_df[!is.na(contrast_tectivirus_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_tectivirus_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ], colnames = c("Gene symbol", "Description", "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE, filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list( dom = "Brftip", buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"), fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2), scrollX = TRUE, scroller = TRUE, deferRender = TRUE)) %>% formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>% formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Volcano plot A volcano plot displays each gene according to its LFC on the x-axis and the statistical significance (−log₁₀ adjusted p-value) on the y-axis. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} volcano_plot(contrast_tectivirus_df, phages = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Boxplots ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} cpm_df <- as.data.frame(cpm(full_counts)) ``` ::: ### GIL01 In GIL01, we found that the most highly expressed gene is GIL01_18, which corresponds to the major capsid protein gene. It is also worth highlighting a de novo transcript identified by StringTie on the negative strand (GIL01-DNT_01), which shows expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL01_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) genes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} gil01_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>% tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>% dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>% tidyr::pivot_longer( cols = starts_with("GIL01_t60-mock"), names_to = "Sample", values_to = "CPM" ) %>% dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1)) gil01_annotation <- annotation %>% dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>% dplyr::mutate( Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)), Color = dplyr::case_when( Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8 ~ "red", Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred", Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey", Strand == "-" ~ "blue", TRUE ~ "black" ) ) gil01_long_cpm <- gil01_long_cpm %>% dplyr::left_join( gil01_annotation %>% dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype), by = "Gene_ID" ) %>% dplyr::mutate( Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol), Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description), Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype), Gene_ID = factor( Gene_ID, levels = gil01_annotation$Gene_ID[ gil01_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID ] ) ) p <- ggplot(gil01_long_cpm, aes( x = Gene_ID, y = CPM_log2, text = paste( "Gene ID:", Gene_ID, ifelse( Gene_symbol != "", paste0("
Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol), "" ), ifelse( Description != "", paste0("
Description: ", Description), "" ), ifelse( Description == "Hypothetical protein", paste0("
Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype), "" ), "
Sample:", Sample, "
Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2) ) )) + geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) + geom_jitter( width = 0.25, size = 1.5, alpha = 0.7, aes(color = Color) ) + theme_minimal() + xlab(NULL) + ylab("Gene expression") + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil01_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) + scale_color_identity() + theme( axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "none" ) ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### GIL16 In the case of GIL16, the results are similar. Again, the major capsid protein gene (GIL16_18) is the most highly expressed. Although there is greater variation between samples in this case, GIL16-DNT_01 exhibits expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL16_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL16_05) genes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} gil16_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>% tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>% dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL16", Gene_ID)) %>% tidyr::pivot_longer( cols = starts_with("GIL16_t60-mock"), names_to = "Sample", values_to = "CPM" ) %>% dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1)) gil16_annotation <- annotation %>% dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL16", Gene_ID)) %>% dplyr::mutate( Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)), Color = dplyr::case_when( Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8 ~ "red", Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred", Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey", Strand == "-" ~ "blue", TRUE ~ "black" ) ) gil16_long_cpm <- gil16_long_cpm %>% dplyr::left_join( gil16_annotation %>% dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype), by = "Gene_ID" ) %>% dplyr::mutate( Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol), Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description), Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype), Gene_ID = factor( Gene_ID, levels = gil16_annotation$Gene_ID[ gil16_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID ] ) ) p <- ggplot(gil16_long_cpm, aes( x = Gene_ID, y = CPM_log2, text = paste( "Gene ID:", Gene_ID, ifelse( Gene_symbol != "", paste0("
Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol), "" ), ifelse( Description != "", paste0("
Description: ", Description), "" ), ifelse( Description == "Hypothetical protein", paste0("
Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype), "" ), "
Sample:", Sample, "
Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2) ) )) + geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) + geom_jitter( width = 0.25, size = 1.5, alpha = 0.7, aes(color = Color) ) + theme_minimal() + xlab(NULL) + ylab("Gene expression") + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil16_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) + scale_color_identity() + theme( axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "none" ) ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Save results ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} create_summary(contrast_name = "Strains GIL16 vs GIL01", rds_name = "gil16_gil01_t60-mock") ``` :::
